title: Ergodicity of the 2D Navier-Stokes equations with degenerate stochastic forcing
overall_summary: Hairer and Mattingly introduce the asymptotic strong Feller property for Markov semigroups on Polish spaces and prove that it rules out two distinct ergodic invariant measures whose supports meet at a point. Their abstract consequence is that asymptotic strong Fellerness plus a weak irreducibility condition, namely the existence of a point lying in the support of every invariant probability measure, implies uniqueness of the invariant measure. They then prove this property for degenerate-noise 2D stochastic Navier-Stokes equations using high-low mode splitting, Malliavin calculus, and an approximate integration by parts formula. For the original SPDE, the most directly reusable content is the abstract uniqueness criterion, the gradient-estimate criterion for asymptotic strong Fellerness, and the support-at-a-common-point argument based on a priori bounds plus accessibility of a small ball around 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.1 and 3.8: totally separating metrics and asymptotic strong Feller property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space. A pseudo-metric d on X is a continuous function d:X×X→R_+ with d(x,x)=0 and satisfying the triangle inequality. An increasing sequence {d_n}_{n≥0} of pseudo-metrics is called totally separating if d_n(x,y)→1 for every pair x≠y. For a pseudo-metric d, define the Lipschitz seminorm ∥φ∥_d=sup_{x≠y}|φ(x)-φ(y)|/d(x,y), and for two positive finite Borel measures µ_1,µ_2 of equal mass define ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d as the infimum of ∫_{X×X} d(x,y) π(dx,dy) over all couplings π of µ_1 and µ_2. A Markov semigroup P_t on X is asymptotically strong Feller at x∈X if there exist a totally separating system {d_n} and times t_n>0 such that inf_{U open, x∈U} limsup_{n→∞} sup_{y∈U} ∥P_{t_n}(x,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}=0. It is asymptotically strong Feller if this holds at every x∈X. If B(x,γ) denotes balls for any metric inducing the topology on X, the condition is equivalently lim_{γ→0} limsup_{n→∞} sup_{y∈B(x,γ)} ∥P_{t_n}(x,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is definitional. The point of the definition is that the distances d_n become increasingly close to the discrete metric 1_{x≠y}, so closeness in ∥·∥_{d_n} for large n is an asymptotic substitute for continuity of transition probabilities in total variation. The equivalent ball formulation follows because neighborhoods of x and metric balls around x form the same local filter in a Polish topology.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: Monge-Kantorovich duality for continuous pseudo-metrics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d be a continuous pseudo-metric on a Polish space X, and let µ_1 and µ_2 be positive finite Borel measures on X with equal mass. For a finite signed measure ν with total mass zero, define |||ν|||_d=sup_{∥φ∥_d=1} ∫_X φ dν. Then ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d=|||µ_1−µ_2|||_d, where ∥·∥_d is the coupling distance associated with d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define an equivalence relation x~y iff d(x,y)=0, and let X_d=X/~ with quotient map π:X→X_d. The pseudo-metric d descends to a genuine separable metric on X_d, though X_d need not be complete. Standard Monge-Kantorovich duality on the separable metric space X_d gives equality between the coupling formulation and the Lipschitz-dual formulation for π_*µ_1 and π_*µ_2. Both the coupling cost and the dual seminorm are unchanged by passing from X to X_d, since d vanishes exactly on equivalence classes and d-Lipschitz functions are precisely pullbacks of Lipschitz functions on the quotient, up to irrelevant constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: monotone convergence of transport distances
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let {d_n}_{n≥0} be a bounded increasing family of continuous pseudo-metrics on a Polish space X, and define d(x,y)=lim_{n→∞} d_n(x,y). If µ_1 and µ_2 are positive measures on X with equal mass, then lim_{n→∞} ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n}=∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since d_n increases and is bounded, the limit L=lim_n ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n} exists and L≤∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d is immediate. For the reverse inequality, choose for each n an optimal coupling π_n for d_n. The family {π_n} is tight because all π_n have the same fixed marginals µ_1 and µ_2; take a weakly convergent subsequence with limit π_∞. For m≥n, optimality gives ∫d_n dπ_m≤∫d_m dπ_m≤L. Passing m→∞ using continuity of d_n yields ∫d_n dπ_∞≤L for every fixed n. Dominated convergence then gives ∫d dπ_∞≤L, so ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d≤L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5: total variation as the limit along totally separating metrics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space and let {d_n} be a totally separating system of pseudo-metrics for X. If µ_1 and µ_2 are positive measures of equal mass on X, then ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{TV}=lim_{n→∞} ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n}, where the paper uses the normalized total variation norm ∥ν∥_{TV}=1/2(ν^+(X)+ν^−(X)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a totally separating system, d_n(x,y) increases pointwise to the discrete cost d(x,y)=1 for x≠y and d(x,x)=0. The optimal coupling cost for this limiting d equals the minimum possible mass off the diagonal, which is exactly the normalized total variation distance. Lemma 3.4 applies because the limiting pseudo-metric need not be continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.10: strong Feller implies asymptotic strong Feller
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a Markov semigroup P_t on a Polish space is strong Feller at some time t>0, then it is asymptotically strong Feller. More precisely, if P_t maps bounded measurable functions to continuous functions, then P_{2t}=P_tP_t has transition probabilities continuous in total variation, and this total-variation continuity implies the asymptotic strong Feller condition with a constant time sequence t_n=2t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the fact that if P and Q are two strong Feller Markov operators on the same Polish space, then the product PQ has transition probabilities continuous in total variation. Applying this with P=Q=P_t gives total-variation continuity of x↦P_{2t}(x,·). Since ∥·∥_{d_n}≤∥·∥_{TV} for the bounded totally separating metrics used in the definition, total-variation continuity immediately yields the required local limsup condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11: gradient criterion for the strong Feller property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Hilbert space H. Suppose that for some fixed time t and every function φ:H→R with finite ∥φ∥_∞ and finite ∥∇φ∥_∞ one has |∇P_tφ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)∥φ∥_∞, where C:R_+→R is a fixed nondecreasing function independent of φ. Then P_t is strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted as a standard criterion from Da Prato and Zabczyk. The essential mechanism is that the derivative estimate depends only on the sup norm of the test function and not on its derivative. One first obtains local Lipschitz continuity of P_tφ for smooth bounded φ with a bound uniform under bounded pointwise approximations. Then bounded measurable functions are approximated by smooth bounded functions, and the Markov property plus dominated convergence pass continuity to P_tφ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12: gradient criterion for asymptotic strong Feller
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Hilbert space. Let {t_n} be a nondecreasing sequence of positive times and let δ_n>0 with δ_n→0. Suppose that for every φ:H→R with finite ∥φ∥_∞ and ∥∇φ∥_∞, the semigroup satisfies |∇P_{t_n}φ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(∥φ∥_∞+δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞) for all n and all x∈H, where C:R_+→R is a fixed nondecreasing function. Then P_t is asymptotically strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For ε>0 define d_ε(w_1,w_2)=1∧ε^{-1}∥w_1−w_2∥, and let ∥·∥_ε be the corresponding Lipschitz and measure seminorm. If δ_n is decreasing, then {d_{δ_n}} is totally separating; otherwise one can pass to a decreasing subsequence without changing the asymptotic conclusion. For a Fréchet differentiable φ with ∥φ∥_ε≤1, constants may be subtracted so that ∥φ∥_∞ is controlled by 1, and ∥∇φ∥_∞≤ε^{-1}. The assumed gradient bound gives |P_{t_n}φ(w_1)−P_{t_n}φ(w_2)|≤∥w_1−w_2∥ C(∥w_1∥∨∥w_2∥)(1+δ_n/ε). General d_ε-Lipschitz φ are approximated pointwise by Fréchet differentiable functions using a linear strong Feller diffusion, preserving the d_ε-Lipschitz seminorm, and dominated convergence passes the estimate to all such φ. Therefore ∥P_{t_n}(w_1,·)−P_{t_n}(w_2,·)∥_ε≤∥w_1−w_2∥ C(∥w_1∥∨∥w_2∥)(1+δ_n/ε). Taking ε=a_n=√δ_n gives a totally separating scale with right side bounded by a local constant times ∥w_1−w_2∥(1+a_n), which tends to 0 as w_2→w_1 uniformly in the required limsup sense.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.16: asymptotic strong Feller separates ergodic invariant supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Polish space X, and let µ and ν be two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for P_t. If P_t is asymptotically strong Feller at x∈X, then x∉supp(µ)∩supp(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Distinct ergodic invariant probability measures are mutually singular, so their normalized total variation distance is 1. The proof first establishes the key inequality: for any measurable A⊂X, any t>0, and any pseudo-metric d≤1, ∥µ−ν∥_d≤1−α(1−M), where α=min{µ(A),ν(A)} and M=max_{y,z∈A}∥P_t(z,·)−P_t(y,·)∥_d. To prove this, decompose µ=(1−α)µ_bar+αµ_A and ν=(1−α)ν_bar+αν_A with µ_A,ν_A supported on A; use invariance P_t^*µ=µ and P_t^*ν=ν; then apply the triangle inequality and the trivial bound ∥·∥_d≤1 to the first part and integrate transition-distance bounds over A×A for the second part. Now assume x lies in both supports. By asymptotic strong Fellerness, choose an open U containing x and N such that for all n>N and all y,z∈U, ∥P_{t_n}(z,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}≤1/2. Since x is in both supports, α=min{µ(U),ν(U)}>0. The inequality gives ∥µ−ν∥_{d_n}≤1−α/2 for all n>N. Corollary 3.5 then implies ∥µ−ν∥_{TV}≤1−α/2, contradicting mutual singularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.17: asymptotic strong Feller plus a common support point gives uniqueness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be an asymptotically strong Feller Markov semigroup on a Polish space X. If there exists a point x∈X such that x∈supp(µ) for every invariant probability measure µ of P_t, then P_t has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If there were two distinct invariant probability measures, their ergodic decompositions would yield at least two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures. The assumed common-support condition applies in particular to all ergodic invariant measures, so both would have x in their supports. This contradicts Theorem 3.16.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation 4.9: approximate integration by parts with a residual error
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes setting of the paper, let w_t be the solution, J_{0,t}ξ the derivative at time t with respect to an initial perturbation ξ, and A_{0,t}v the Malliavin derivative at time t induced by an infinitesimal Cameron-Martin perturbation v of the Wiener path over [0,t]. For a possibly nonadapted process v, set ρ_t=J_{0,t}ξ−A_{0,t}v. Then for smooth φ and P_tφ(w)=E_w φ(w_t), ⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩=E_w[φ(w_t)∫_0^t v(s)dW(s)]+E_w[(∇φ)(w_t)ρ_t], where the stochastic integral is Itô if v is adapted and Skorokhod if v is nonadapted. Consequently |⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩|≤∥φ∥_∞ E_w|∫_0^t v(s)dW(s)|+∥∇φ∥_∞ E_w∥ρ_t∥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from ⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩=E[(∇φ)(w_t)J_{0,t}ξ]. Decompose J_{0,t}ξ=A_{0,t}v+ρ_t. By the Malliavin chain rule, E[(∇φ)(w_t)A_{0,t}v]=E[D^v φ(w_t)], where D^v is the Malliavin derivative in direction v. Malliavin integration by parts gives E[D^v φ(w_t)]=E[φ(w_t)∫v dW], with Skorokhod interpretation when v is nonadapted. The final inequality is immediate from the sup norms. The estimate is the paper's central replacement for exact Bismut-Elworthy-Li formulas when the chosen noise perturbation only cancels the initial perturbation asymptotically.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1: asymptotic strong Feller property for the restricted 2D Navier-Stokes dynamics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the two-dimensional stochastic Navier-Stokes equation in vorticity form on the torus, driven by a finite-dimensional additive noise acting on Fourier modes Z_0, let H_tilde be the closed subspace spanned by the Fourier modes in the nonlinear closure Z_tilde_∞ of Z_0. Then the Markov transition semigroup on H_tilde generated by the solutions is asymptotically strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces Theorem 4.1 to Proposition 4.3. Proposition 4.3 gives, for integer times n, a derivative estimate of the form ∥∇P_nφ(w)∥≤C exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+e^{−δ n}∥∇φ∥_∞). This is exactly the hypothesis of Proposition 3.12 with t_n=n and δ_n=e^{−δ n}, after absorbing exp(η∥w∥^2) into the local nondecreasing coefficient C(∥w∥). Therefore the semigroup is asymptotically strong Feller on H_tilde.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3: derivative estimate implying asymptotic strong Feller for Navier-Stokes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes setting restricted to H_tilde, for every η>0 there exist constants C>0 and δ>0 such that every Fréchet differentiable φ:H_tilde→R satisfies ∥∇P_nφ(w)∥≤C exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+∥∇φ∥_∞ e^{−δ n}) for every w∈H_tilde and every integer n≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a unit direction ξ∈H_tilde. The proof constructs an infinitesimal perturbation v of the Wiener path so that the residual ρ_n=J_{0,n}ξ−A_{0,n}v_{0,n} decays exponentially in moments, while the Skorokhod cost of v remains bounded. Time is split into unit intervals, and on the first half of each interval [n,n+1/2] the control is chosen as v_n=A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1} J_hat_n ρ_n, where A_n is the Malliavin map on that half interval, M_n=A_nA_n^* is the Malliavin matrix, J_hat_n is the derivative flow over the first half interval, and β>0 regularizes the inverse. On [n+1/2,n+1] the control is zero, allowing parabolic dissipation to damp high modes. This gives the recursion ρ_{n+1}=J_check_n β(β+M_n)^{−1} J_hat_n ρ_n. The Malliavin lower-tail estimate of Theorem 4.12, high-mode damping, and a small choice of β yield Proposition 4.13: E∥ρ_N∥^{10}≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2)2^{−N}. A Skorokhod-isometry calculation using Malliavin derivatives of A_n and J_hat_n yields Proposition 4.14: the square cost of ∫v dW is controlled by a convergent series in the residual moments. Plugging these two bounds into the approximate integration by parts estimate, with N=n, gives the displayed derivative bound uniformly over ∥ξ∥=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: uniqueness of the invariant measure on H_tilde for the Navier-Stokes application
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation considered in the paper, restricted to the invariant subspace H_tilde generated by the forced modes and the nonlinearity, there exists exactly one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is standard for this dissipative SPDE. Uniqueness follows from Corollary 3.17. Theorem 4.1 supplies asymptotic strong Fellerness. It remains to show that every invariant measure has 0 in its support. Applying Itô's formula to the enstrophy ∥w∥^2 and using invariance gives the uniform a priori bound ∫_H ∥w∥^2 µ(dw)≤C E_0/ν for every invariant measure µ, where E_0=tr QQ^*. Hence there is a radius C_tilde such that µ(B(C_tilde))>1/2 for every invariant µ. A controllability or small-ball lemma from E and Mattingly, whose proof also works for the full solution, states that for every γ>0 there is T_γ such that inf_{w∈B(C_tilde)} P_{T_γ}(w,B(γ))>0. Invariance then gives µ(B(γ))≥∫_{B(C_tilde)}P_{T_γ}(w,B(γ))µ(dw)>0 for every γ>0. Thus every neighborhood of 0 has positive µ-mass, so 0∈supp(µ) for every invariant µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11: essentially elliptic gradient estimate via adapted low-mode control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be the semigroup generated by the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation on H. There exists N_* depending on the energy injection E_0 and viscosity ν such that, if the forced set Z_0 contains every Fourier mode k with 0<|k|≤N_*, then for every η>0 there are constants c,γ>0 such that |∇P_tφ(w)|≤c exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+e^{−γt}∥∇φ∥_∞) for all suitable φ and all t≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a low-mode projection π_l onto modes below a cutoff N and high-mode projection π_h=1−π_l, with N large enough that high modes are strongly dissipative and with π_lH contained in the range of the noise operator Q. Given an initial derivative direction ξ, define an auxiliary process ζ_t by ∂_tζ_t=−(1/2)π_lζ_t/∥π_lζ_t∥+νΔπ_hζ_t+π_h B_tilde(w_t,ζ_t), ζ_0=ξ, with 0/0 interpreted as 0. Then the low part ζ_t^l reaches 0 by time at most 2, while the high part obeys the linearized dissipative dynamics. Define an adapted control v(t)=Q_l^{−1}F_t with F_t=(1/2)ζ_t^l/∥ζ_t^l∥+νΔζ_t^l+π_l B_tilde(w_t,ζ_t), so that the residual ρ_t in the approximate integration by parts formula equals ζ_t. Energy estimates for the Jacobian and the Navier-Stokes solution give E∥ζ_t^h∥^p≤C e^{η∥w_0∥^2}e^{−γt}. Because v is adapted, the cost E|∫v dW| is bounded by Itô isometry and estimates on F_t. Substitution into Equation 4.9 yields the derivative bound. The construction shows how direct forcing of sufficiently many low modes plus dissipation of high modes can replace full ellipticity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10: a priori bounds for the Navier-Stokes solution, Jacobian, and Hessian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the 2D stochastic Navier-Stokes vorticity equation in the paper, let w_t be the solution, E_0=tr QQ^*, ∥·∥ the L^2 norm, and ∥·∥_1 the H^1 norm. The paper proves: (1) There are C,η_0>0 depending only on Q and ν such that for every s≥0 and η≤η_0, E exp(η sup_{t≥s}(∥w_t∥^2+ν∫_s^t∥w_r∥_1^2 dr−E_0(t−s)))≤C exp(η e^{−νs}∥w_0∥^2). (2) There are η_1,a,γ>0 depending only on E_0 and ν such that E exp(η Σ_{n=0}^N∥w_n∥^2−γN)≤exp(aη∥w_0∥^2) for every N and η≤η_1. (3) For every η>0 there is C such that the derivative flow J_{0,t} satisfies ∥J_{0,t}∥≤exp(η∫_0^t∥w_s∥_1^2 ds+Ct) almost surely. (4) For every η>0 and p>0 there is C such that the second derivative flow K_{s,t} satisfies E∥K_{s,t}∥^p≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2) for all s>0 and t∈(s,s+1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The solution estimates start from Itô's formula for ∥w_t∥^2. The Navier-Stokes nonlinearity is energy-conserving in vorticity form, so the drift contributes the dissipative term −ν∥w_t∥_1^2, while the noise contributes a martingale ∫⟨w,QdW⟩ and trace E_0t. An exponential martingale estimate gives the pathwise exponential bound and, by iteration over integer times plus the one-time exponential moment estimate, the discrete exponential moment bound. For the Jacobian, write the linearized equation ∂_tξ_t=νΔξ_t+B_tilde(w_t,ξ_t). Sobolev and interpolation inequalities bound the nonlinear term by ν∥ξ_t∥_1^2 plus η∥w_t∥_1^2∥ξ_t∥^2 and constants, after which Gronwall gives the displayed bound. For the Hessian, use the variation-of-constants formula K_{s,t}(ξ,ξ')=∫_s^t J_{r,t}B_tilde(J_{s,r}ξ,J_{s,r}ξ')dr, Sobolev estimates on B_tilde, smoothing estimates for J, and the previous exponential integrability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12: Malliavin matrix lower-tail estimate quoted from Mattingly-Pardoux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the Malliavin matrix for the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation over the time interval [0,1/2], and let H_tilde be the invariant subspace generated by the forced modes. For every α>0, η>0, p>0, and every orthogonal projection π_l onto finitely many Fourier modes, there exists C_tilde such that P(⟨Mφ,φ⟩<ε∥φ∥_1^2)≤C_tilde ε^p exp(η∥w_0∥^2) for every random vector φ∈H_tilde satisfying ∥π_lφ∥≥α∥φ∥_1 almost surely, every ε∈(0,1), and every initial condition w_0∈H_tilde.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Mattingly and Pardoux rather than proving it. It says that, with high probability, the Malliavin covariance is nondegenerate in directions that have a nontrivial low-mode component. In the proof of Proposition 4.3 it is used to show that applying β(β+M)^{−1} to such a direction either makes it very small or pushes its mass into high modes, where deterministic dissipation can contract it.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.17: high-mode damping for the Jacobian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Navier-Stokes derivative flow J_{0,T}, for every p≥1, T>0, and γ,η>0, there exists an orthogonal projection π_l onto finitely many Fourier modes such that E∥(1−π_l)J_{0,T}∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2) and E∥J_{0,T}(1−π_l)∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first estimate, the paper proves a smoothing bound for the Jacobian: ∥J_tξ∥_1^2≤C t^{−1} exp(η∫_0^1∥w_s∥_1^2ds)∥ξ∥^2 for t∈(0,1]. A high-mode projection π_N satisfies ∥π_Nξ∥≤N^{−1}∥ξ∥_1, so choosing N large and using the exponential integrability of ∫∥w_s∥_1^2 gives the bound. For the second estimate, decompose ξ_t into low and high parts. The high part has a term −νN^2∥ξ_t^h∥^2 from the Laplacian, plus lower-order couplings controlled by ∥w_t∥_{1/2}; the low part starts at zero and is generated only through coupling to the high part. Gronwall, interpolation, exponential energy estimates, and a sufficiently large cutoff N make both contributions arbitrarily small in L^p up to the factor exp(η∥w_0∥^2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.18: regularized Malliavin inverse kills low-mode components
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix ξ∈H_tilde and define ζ=β(β+M_0)^{−1}J_hat_0ξ, where M_0 is the Malliavin matrix on [0,1/2], J_hat_0 is the derivative flow over [0,1/2], and β>0. For every γ,η>0, every p≥1, and every finite-dimensional low-mode projection π_l, there exists β>0 such that E∥π_lζ∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2)∥ξ∥^p.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let A_α be the event ∥π_lζ∥>α∥ζ∥_1, and split ζ=ζ_α+ζ_bar_α according to A_α and its complement. On the complement, ∥π_lζ_bar_α∥≤α∥ζ∥_1, and the Jacobian bounds allow α to be chosen so small that this part has the required expectation. On A_α, the vector ζ_α has a significant low-mode component, so Theorem 4.12 applies. The identity ⟨ζ,M_0ζ⟩≤β∥J_hat_0ξ∥^2 follows from ζ=β(β+M_0)^{−1}J_hat_0ξ. Combining this with the Malliavin lower-tail estimate gives high inverse-moment bounds of the form E[(∥ζ_α∥_1/∥J_hat_0ξ∥)^p]≤Cβ^q exp(η∥w_0∥^2). Choosing β small then makes E∥π_lζ_α∥^p arbitrarily small, and the two pieces together give the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.16: one-step contraction of the controlled residual
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the residual recursion ρ_{n+1}=J_check_n β(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n used in the proof of Proposition 4.3, for every γ,η>0 and every p≥1 there exists β_0>0 such that, whenever β≤β_0, E(∥ρ_{n+1}∥^p | F_n)≤γ exp(η∥w_n∥^2)∥ρ_n∥^p almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set ζ_n=β(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n, so ρ_{n+1}=J_check_nζ_n. Basic Jacobian estimates give E(∥ζ_n∥^p|F_n)≤C exp((η/2)∥w_n∥^2)∥ρ_n∥^p uniformly in β. Choose a finite-dimensional projection π_l using Lemma 4.17 so that J_check_n(1−π_l) is small in conditional L^p; this controls the high-mode part of ζ_n after the second half-step. Then Lemma 4.18, with β small, makes the low-mode part π_lζ_n small before applying J_check_n. A final Jacobian estimate propagates the low part through J_check_n. Combining the two contributions yields the stated conditional contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.13: exponential decay of the residual error
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 4.3, for any η>0 there exist β>0 and C>0 such that E∥ρ_N∥^{10}≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2)2^{−N} for every integer N≥0. The paper notes that by decreasing β further, the factor 2^N can be replaced by K^N for any fixed K≥2, without changing C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C_n=∥ρ_{n+1}∥^{10}/∥ρ_n∥^{10}, with C_n=0 if ρ_n=0. Since ρ_0 has unit norm, ∥ρ_N∥^{10}=Π_{n=0}^{N−1}C_n. A crude Jacobian bound gives C_n≤exp(η∫_n^{n+1}∥w_s∥_1^2ds+C_η), uniformly in β. For a large R define a truncated-renormalized variable C_{n,R}=e^{−ηR} if ∥w_n∥^2≥2R and C_{n,R}=e^{ηR}C_n otherwise. Lemma 4.16 allows β to be chosen so that E(C_{n,R}^2|F_n)≤1/2. The crude bound also implies C_n≤C_{n,R} exp(η∫_n^{n+1}∥w_s∥_1^2ds+η∥w_n∥^2+C_η−ηR). Multiplying over n and using the inequality that a product is controlled by the product of C_{n,R}^2 plus exponential energy terms, the first contribution decays by the conditional second-moment bound, while the remaining energy contributions are controlled by Lemma 4.10. Choosing R large and then β small yields the exponential decay.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14: Skorokhod cost bound for the nonadapted control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the nonadapted control v constructed in Proposition 4.3 and any η>0, there is C such that E|∫_0^N v(s)dW(s)|^2≤(C/β^2) exp(η∥w_0∥^2) Σ_{n=0}^∞ (E∥ρ_n∥^{10})^{1/5}, uniformly in N. The integral is a Skorokhod integral because v is generally nonadapted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Skorokhod isometry: E|∫_0^N v dW|^2 is bounded by E∥v∥_{L^2(0,N)}^2 plus the double integral over Malliavin derivatives D_s v_n(t) on each controlled half-interval [n,n+1/2]. The control is v_n=A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n. Since ρ_n is F_n-measurable, its Malliavin derivative vanishes for s≥n. Differentiating v_n requires differentiating A_n, A_n^*, M_n=A_nA_n^*, and J_hat_n. The derivative of the inverse is D(β+M_n)^{−1}=−(β+M_n)^{−1}(DM_n)(β+M_n)^{−1}. The operator inequalities ∥A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1/2}∥≤1, ∥(β+M_n)^{−1/2}A_n∥≤1, and ∥(β+M_n)^{−1/2}∥≤β^{−1/2} yield ∥D_s v_n∥≤3β^{−1}∥D_sA_n∥∥J_hat_n∥∥ρ_n∥+β^{−1/2}∥D_sJ_hat_n∥∥ρ_n∥. Malliavin derivative formulas express D_sA_n and D_sJ_hat_n through the Hessian K and Jacobian J. Lemma 4.10 bounds the resulting moments, and Hölder gives E∥D_s v_n∥^2≤C e^{η∥w_0∥^2}β^{−2}(E∥ρ_n∥^{10})^{1/5}. The same type of bound controls E∥v_n∥^2, and summing over n gives the result.

proof_techniques.name: Asymptotic strong Feller uniqueness criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the classical strong Feller plus irreducibility route by a weaker asymptotic condition. The key is to measure transition probabilities using a sequence of Wasserstein-type distances associated with pseudo-metrics d_n that increase to the discrete metric. If the semigroup asymptotically makes transition laws from nearby initial points close in these increasingly separating distances, then two distinct ergodic invariant measures cannot both have the same point in their supports, because Corollary 3.5 would force their total variation distance to be strictly less than 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, it would be enough to prove that P_t is asymptotically strong Feller and that all invariant measures have a common support point, such as 0. This bypasses the need to prove full topological irreducibility or ordinary strong Feller if the singular Dirac drift makes those difficult.

proof_techniques.name: Gradient estimate with a decaying derivative-loss term
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.12 shows that a bound of the form |∇P_{t_n}φ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(∥φ∥_∞+δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞), with δ_n→0, is sufficient for asymptotic strong Fellerness. The ∥φ∥_∞ term represents genuine probabilistic smoothing, while the δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞ term represents a residual unsmoothed component that is damped by long-time dynamics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the stochastic heat equation with additive noise, one can try to prove this estimate directly, or first for smooth approximating drifts G_{1/n} and then pass to the limiting semigroup. The heat operator's high-mode dissipation is naturally suited to making the derivative-loss term decay.

proof_techniques.name: Common support point from Lyapunov bounds plus small-ball accessibility
proof_techniques.description: In Corollary 4.2, the authors prove that 0 belongs to the support of every invariant measure by combining a uniform invariant-measure moment bound with a positive-probability reachability estimate. The moment bound ensures that every invariant measure assigns uniformly positive mass to a fixed bounded ball. A separate controllability/small-ball result says that from any point in that ball, the process has positive probability to enter any prescribed neighborhood of 0 after some time. Invariance then forces every invariant measure to charge every neighborhood of 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, an analogous plan is to establish a Lyapunov or moment bound under invariant measures and a support or small-noise argument showing positive probability of reaching a neighborhood of 0 from bounded initial data. This would verify the weak irreducibility hypothesis of Corollary 3.17.

proof_techniques.name: Approximate integration by parts instead of exact Bismut formula
proof_techniques.description: The paper derives an identity in which an initial-condition derivative is partially represented by a Malliavin derivative in the noise direction, leaving a residual ρ_t. The derivative of P_tφ is bounded by a noise-shift cost involving ∫v dW and by ∥∇φ∥∞ times E∥ρ_t∥. If v is chosen so that the residual decays as t→∞ and the noise-shift cost remains bounded, the estimate has exactly the structure needed for asymptotic strong Fellerness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the singular drift prevents a clean strong Feller proof, one can try to construct an approximate control v for the regularized equations and obtain residual decay from the heat semigroup. The assumed convergence of regularized solutions may then help transfer the resulting smoothing or ASF bounds to the limiting δ_0 drift equation.

proof_techniques.name: High-low mode splitting
proof_techniques.description: The Navier-Stokes proof separates finitely many low modes, where randomness or Malliavin covariance must provide smoothing, from high modes, where the Laplacian gives strong deterministic contraction. This splitting appears both in the adapted essentially elliptic argument and in the hypoelliptic regularized-Malliavin-inverse construction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The stochastic heat equation has an even more explicit spectral gap and high-mode damping under Dirichlet boundary conditions. A proof for the original problem may only need to control finitely many low Dirichlet modes probabilistically while using heat dissipation for the rest.

proof_techniques.name: Adapted control in the essentially elliptic case
proof_techniques.description: When all sufficiently many low modes are directly forced, Proposition 4.11 constructs an adapted Cameron-Martin shift that kills the low-mode part of an initial perturbation in finite time. Since the control is adapted, the cost is estimated by the ordinary Itô isometry rather than by Skorokhod calculus. High modes then decay exponentially by dissipation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Space-time white noise is nondegenerate in all Dirichlet modes, so an adapted-control or Bismut-type argument may be feasible for smooth approximations of the original SPDE. This could yield strong Feller or at least the gradient estimate in Proposition 3.12.

proof_techniques.name: Regularized Malliavin inverse for degenerate or nonadapted smoothing
proof_techniques.description: In the fully hypoelliptic Navier-Stokes argument, the authors avoid assuming that the Malliavin matrix is globally invertible. They use (β+M)^{−1} with β>0 on short time intervals, accepting an error β(β+M)^{−1} that is then shown to be either small in low modes or shifted to high modes. The construction is nonadapted, so Skorokhod integral estimates and Malliavin derivatives of the control are required.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the limiting δ_0 drift equation lacks a tractable global Malliavin inverse, a regularized inverse on finite-dimensional projections may still be enough. This is most relevant if one proves uniform estimates for the smooth G_{1/n} approximations and cannot pass exact strong Feller estimates to the limit.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller as a sufficient route through ASF
proof_techniques.description: The paper explicitly notes that strong Feller semigroups on Polish spaces are asymptotically strong Feller after composing the transition operator with itself. Therefore all ASF uniqueness results can be used even if one proves ordinary strong Feller by more classical methods.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the ABLM24 framework or the G_{1/n} approximations can be used to prove strong Feller for P_t, then the paper's Corollary 3.17 reduces uniqueness to the common-support-point condition.

other_useful_info: The paper's weak irreducibility condition is much weaker than full topological irreducibility: it only requires a point belonging to the support of every invariant measure. Examples 3.13-3.15 show that asymptotic strong Fellerness can hold even when strong Fellerness fails, including an infinite-dimensional Ornstein-Uhlenbeck example where transition laws from different initial data can be singular at finite times but the deterministic contraction still gives ASF. The proof of Corollary 4.2 uses a small-ball/accessibility lemma from E and Mattingly 2001 and standard invariant-measure energy estimates; this pattern is likely more transferable to dissipative SPDEs than the paper's Fourier-geometry analysis for Navier-Stokes. The paper cites Da Prato-Zabczyk for classical strong Feller/ergodicity tools, Hairer 2002 for asymptotic coupling, and Mattingly-Pardoux 2006 for the Malliavin matrix lower-tail estimate used in the hypoelliptic proof.
why_relevant: This paper introduces the asymptotic strong Feller property and proves that it plus weak irreducibility yields uniqueness of the invariant measure. The abstract criterion is useful if the singular drift prevents proving ordinary strong Feller for Pt.
